title: Respective Messages and Meanings shared
wikitext:
[]: Respective Messages & Meanings shared             Quote Post by Archaea » Sun Jun 20, 2010 8:43 pm Currently this thread doesn’t contain any spoilers- but it probably will in the future. Be wary The Subject of the thread is dedicated to the messages found in Neon Genesis Evangelion… and the Rebuild . For this thread EOE will constitute NGE. Actually, anything found within the vast Evangelion Canon is fair game here. I think that great art (and possibly all art for that matter) is ambiguous and therefore holds different, unique messages and meaning for each individual who experiences it. Without a doubt, Neon Genesis Evangelion is just that. -Ambiguous Art- One could ask what this or that event meant, or to what the possible symbolism of X interacting with Y pertains- The fact is, NGE holds many messages for us all. In lieu of scrutinizing every “important” event and expressing every feeling felt, I am simply asking what are some of the larger-picture messages that you feel NGE was trying to convey (or that you perhaps extrapolated from it), and/or what is something that you have taken from the show and thought about applying to (or have already implemented in) your daily life? philosophies, ideas, ect. ME: Personally- I haven’t really reflected enough on it yet, as I have only relatively recently seen and finished the series in its entirety for the first time. But one of the main messages that I chose to see (or maybe someone in the series actually said it) was that anyone can be happy no matter what their circumstance. Everything is a matter of perspective. almost solipsistic stuff… And to supplement and give some accreditation to this thread, I’ll add a quote from the creator of Gundam. In the thread “Gundam Creator Yoshiyuki Tomino's thoughts on Eva” Holy Diver presents a quotation from Gundam Creator Yoshiyuki Tomino. [How about that for redundancy!] there ellipses at play. Holy Diver wrote: I found this on /m/ and I'm not quite sure what to make of this. Specifically, I missed the point in Eva that told me to start shooting other people. “…Instead they tried to convince the audience to admit that everybody is sick, practically in the middle of a nervous breakdown, all the time… it's much more interested in admitting that we're all depressed nervous wrecks, I thought. It was a work that told people it was okay to be depressed, and it accepted the psychological state that said if you don't like the way the world works, then it's okay to just pick up a gun and attack someone.”

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jun 20, 2010 9:09 pm I'm pretty sure NGE is all about the mayo. And teh Rei.

[]:             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Sun Jun 20, 2010 9:41 pm I must say I like this thread much better than your last one. Hopefully we can use this to discuss the themes of NGE in more general terms and in a more personal way. Anyway, I think the messages Anno was trying to convey with NGE went sailing right over Tomino-san's head. If the only message he got out of it was that 'everything can be solved with violence,' well, obviously he wasn't paying much attention. Anno obviously had lots of messages to convey through the series: the damaging nature of gender roles in society, the futility of war/violence, fear of intimacy, etc. But I want to talk about the overarching message of the entire series, or 'the whole point' as one might call it.In the TV series, the end consists of the main characters, primarily Shinji, coming to terms with and learning to accept and love themselves. The latter is crucial to overcoming depression, of which Anno himself was battling at the time(yeah, I know, master of the obvious). So, to water it down and say it in the most simple way possible, I think the message Anno was trying to convey overall with the series was "Life sucks, but it's worth living anyway." EDIT: Also, I'd like to add to Yojimbo's contribution by mentioning the butt crease in Rei's plugsuit. That's important, too.

[]:             Quote Post by Archaea » Tue Jun 22, 2010 11:37 pm Killer Bee wrote: Anno obviously had lots of messages to convey through the series: the damaging nature of gender roles in society, the futility of war/violence, fear of intimacy, etc. But I want to talk about the overarching message of the entire series, or 'the whole point' as one might call it.In the TV series, the end consists of the main characters, primarily Shinji, coming to terms with and learning to accept and love themselves. The latter is crucial to overcoming depression, of which Anno himself was battling at the time(yeah, I know, master of the obvious). So, to water it down and say it in the most simple way possible, I think the message Anno was trying to convey overall with the series was "Life sucks, but it's worth living anyway."

[]:             Quote Post by 0x2eleven » Wed Jun 23, 2010 12:57 am Evangelion means different things to me each time I experience it, but the one thing that never changes is that I'm able to find comfort in the story and the characters. No matter how messed up I am on the inside, someone in the world knows just how I feel, and even more people have survived the feeling. In creating Evangelion and in the mythos of Evangelion itself, Anno shows that anyone can overcome any barrier, whether it be internal or external, and go on to live a happy and maybe even extraordinary life. I watch and obsess over Evangelion because it makes me feel understood. Is it crazy for me to hope that I'll one day wake-up with everyone who matters to me standing around my bed clapping and congratulating me for having a life-altering epiphany? I can only hope that when I finally do outgrow my own probl...circumstances, I don't also outgrow the ability to appreciate this deeply affecting work of art. Skip telling me I sound like a bleeding heart.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jun 23, 2010 1:18 am 0x2eleven wrote: I can only hope that when I finally do outgrow my own probl...circumstances, I don't also outgrow the ability to appreciate this deeply affecting work of art.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jun 23, 2010 3:11 am FWIW, I think I said everything important (to me) pertaining to this topic HERE.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Jun 23, 2010 3:32 am Mr. Tines wrote: Coming to the series in my mid-40s, I found it could evoke issues that I'd thought were resolved a couple of decades before -- the real difference is whether familiarity would dull your responses, compared with the sudden surprise that is the first watch through.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jun 23, 2010 3:44 am AuraTwilight wrote: ...Since when were you 52? I call shenanigans.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Jun 23, 2010 8:36 am Archaea wrote: Also where did you get the "futility of war and violence"-- I'm not refuting your claim at all, I'm just curious of what examples you are using..

[]:             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Wed Jun 23, 2010 6:12 pm Archaea wrote: Also where did you get the "futility of war and violence"-- I'm not refuting your claim at all, I'm just curious of what examples you are using..

[]:             Quote Post by 0x2eleven » Thu Jun 24, 2010 3:56 am Mr. Tines wrote: Coming to the series in my mid-40s, I found it could evoke issues that I'd thought were resolved a couple of decades before -- the real difference is whether familiarity would dull your responses, compared with the sudden surprise that is the first watch through.
